title: The Merrimac
content:
ï؟½The Indians speak of a beautiful river, far to the south,
which they call Merrimac.ï؟½ - SIEUR. DE MONTS, 1604.


آ آ آ آ Stream of my fathers! sweetly still
آ آ آ آ The sunset rays thy valley fill;
آ آ آ آ Poured slantwise down the long defile,
آ آ آ آ Wave, wood, and spire beneath them smile.
آ آ آ آ I see the winding Powow fold
آ آ آ آ The green hill in its belt of gold,
آ آ آ آ And following down its wavy line,
آ آ آ آ Its sparkling waters blend with thine.
آ آ آ آ There ï؟½s not a tree upon thy side,
آ آ آ آ Nor rock, which thy returning tide
آ آ آ آ As yet hath left abrupt and stark
آ آ آ آ Above thy evening water-mark;
آ آ آ آ No calm cove with its rocky hem,
آ آ آ آ No isle whose emerald swells begin
آ آ آ آ Thy broad, smooth current; not a sail
آ آ آ آ Bowed to the freshening ocean gale;
آ آ آ آ No small boat with its busy oars,
آ آ آ آ Nor gray wall sloping to thy shores;
آ آ آ آ Nor farm-house with its maple shade,
آ آ آ آ Or rigid poplar colonnade,
آ آ آ آ But lies distinct and full in sight,
آ آ آ آ Beneath this gush of sunset light.
آ آ آ آ Centuries ago, that harbor-bar,
آ آ آ آ Stretching its length of foam afar,
آ آ آ آ And Salisburyï؟½s beach of shining sand,
آ آ آ آ And yonder islandï؟½s wave-smoothed strand,
آ آ آ آ Saw the adventurerï؟½s tiny sail,
آ آ آ آ Flit, stooping from the eastern gale;
آ آ آ آ And oï؟½er these woods and waters broke
آ آ آ آ The cheer from Britainï؟½s hearts of oak,
آ آ آ آ As brightly on the voyagerï؟½s eye,
آ آ آ آ Weary of forest, sea, and sky,
آ آ آ آ Breaking the dull continuous wood,
آ آ آ آ The Merrimac rolled down his flood;
آ آ آ آ Mingling that clear pellucid brook,
آ آ آ آ Which channels vast Agioochook
آ آ آ آ When spring-timeï؟½s sun and shower unlock
آ آ آ آ The frozen fountains of the rock,
آ آ آ آ And more abundant waters given
آ آ آ آ From that pure lake, ï؟½The Smile of Heaven,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Tributes from vale and mountain-side,
آ آ آ آ With oceanï؟½s dark, eternal tide!

آ آ آ آ On yonder rocky cape, which braves
آ آ آ آ The stormy challenge of the waves,
آ آ آ آ Midst tangled vine and dwarfish wood,
آ آ آ آ The hardy Anglo-Saxon stood,
آ آ آ آ Planting upon the topmost crag
آ آ آ آ The staff of Englandï؟½s battle-flag;
آ آ آ آ And, while from out its heavy fold
آ آ آ آ Saint Georgeï؟½s crimson cross unrolled,
آ آ آ آ Midst roll of drum and trumpet blare,
آ آ آ آ And weapons brandishing in air,
آ آ آ آ He gave to that lone promontory
آ آ آ آ The sweetest name in all his story;
آ آ آ آ Of her, the flower of Islamï؟½s daughters,
آ آ آ آ Whose harems look on Stamboulï؟½s waters,
آ آ آ آ Who, when the chance of war had bound
آ آ آ آ The Moslem chain his limbs around,
آ آ آ آ Wreathed oï؟½er with silk that iron chain,
آ آ آ آ Soothed with her smiles his hours of pain,
آ آ آ آ And fondly to her youthful slave
آ آ آ آ A dearer gift than freedom gave.

آ آ آ آ But look! the yellow light no more
آ آ آ آ Streams down on wave and verdant shore;
آ آ آ آ And clearly on the calm air swells
آ آ آ آ The twilight voice of distant bells.
آ آ آ آ From Oceanï؟½s bosom, white and thin,
آ آ آ آ The mists come slowly rolling in;
آ آ آ آ Hills, woods, the riverï؟½s rocky rim,
آ آ آ آ Amidst the sea like vapor swim,
آ آ آ آ While yonder lonely coast-light, set
آ آ آ آ Within its wave-washed minaret,
آ آ آ آ Half quenched, a beamless star and pale,
آ آ آ آ Shines dimly through its cloudy veil!

آ آ آ آ Home of my fathers! I have stood
آ آ آ آ Where Hudson rolled his lordly flood
آ آ آ آ Seen sunrise rest and sunset fade
آ آ آ آ Along his frowning Palisade;
آ آ آ آ Looked down the Appalachian peak
آ آ آ آ On Juniataï؟½s silver streak;
آ آ آ آ Have seen along his valley gleam
آ آ آ آ The Mohawkï؟½s softly winding stream;
آ آ آ آ The level light of sunset shine
آ آ آ آ Through broad Potomacï؟½s hem of pine;
آ آ آ آ And autumnï؟½s rainbow-tinted banner
آ آ آ آ Hang lightly oï؟½er the Susquehanna;
آ آ آ آ Yet wheresoeï؟½er his step might be,
آ آ آ آ Thy wandering child looked back to thee!
آ آ آ آ Heard in his dreams thy riverï؟½s sound
آ آ آ آ Of murmuring on its pebbly bound,
آ آ آ آ The unforgotten swell and roar
آ آ آ آ Of waves on thy familiar shore;
آ آ آ آ And saw, amidst the curtained gloom
آ آ آ آ And quiet of his lonely room,
آ آ آ آ Thy sunset scenes before him pass;
آ آ آ آ As, in Agrippaï؟½s magic glass,
آ آ آ آ The loved and lost arose to view,
آ آ آ آ Remembered groves in greenness grew,
آ آ آ آ Bathed still in childhoodï؟½s morning dew,
آ آ آ آ Along whose bowers of beauty swept
آ آ آ آ Whatever Memoryï؟½s mourners wept,
آ آ آ آ Sweet faces, which the charnel kept,
آ آ آ آ Young, gentle eyes, which long had slept;
آ آ آ آ And while the gazer leaned to trace,
آ آ آ آ More near, some dear familiar face,
آ آ آ آ He wept to find the vision flown,
آ آ آ آ A phantom and a dream alone!
meaning:
This evocative piece by John Greenleaf Whittier represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of John Greenleaf Whittier's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
